solution: By the product rule, \( \log_b M + \log_b N = \log_b(MN) \). Since \( [[M]] = [[b]]^{[[p]]} \) and \( [[N]] = [[b]]^{[[q]]} \), each log is an exponent and the sum is \( [[p]] + [[q]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies the two logarithms; the product rule adds them.
This subtracts the exponents, which corresponds to a quotient, not a product.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

solution: By the quotient rule, \( \log_b M - \log_b N = \log_b(M/N) \). Since \( [[M]] = [[b]]^{[[p]]} \) and \( [[N]] = [[b]]^{[[q]]} \), the difference of the exponents is \( [[p]] - [[q]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies the two exponents; the quotient rule subtracts them.
This adds the exponents, which corresponds to a product, not a quotient.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

solution: By the power rule, \( \log_b(M^{k}) = k \log_b M \). Since \( [[M]] = [[b]]^{[[p]]} \), \( \log_{[[b]]}[[M]] = [[p]] \), so the answer is \( [[k]] \cdot [[p]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the exponent \( k \) instead of multiplying it with \( \log_b M \).
This reports only the outer exponent \( k \), ignoring \( \log_b M = [[p]] \).
This reports only \( \log_b M \), forgetting to multiply by the outer exponent \( k \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

solution: The \( n \)-th root rule gives \( \log_b\left(\sqrt[n]{M}\right) = \dfrac{1}{n}\log_b M \). Since \( [[M]] = [[b]]^{[[p]] \cdot [[n]]} \), \( \log_{[[b]]}[[M]] = [[p]]\cdot[[n]] \), so dividing by \( [[n]] \) gives \( [[p]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies by \( n \) instead of dividing by it, undoing the root incorrectly.
This reports the root index itself rather than the simplified logarithm.
This adds the root index instead of dividing by it.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

solution: By the change-of-base formula with common base \( [[c]] \), \( \log_{[[a]]}([[M]]) = \dfrac{\log_{[[c]]}([[M]])}{\log_{[[c]]}([[a]])} = \dfrac{[[q]]}{[[p]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This inverts the ratio, dividing the base's exponent by the argument's instead of the reverse.
This subtracts the exponents, as if using the quotient rule instead of changing base.
This multiplies the exponents instead of dividing them.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

solution: Using the power, product, and quotient rules together: \( 2\log_{[[b]]}[[M]] = 2([[p]]) \), \( \log_{[[b]]}[[N]] = [[q]] \), \( \log_{[[b]]}[[P]] = [[r]] \), so the expression equals \( 2([[p]]) + [[q]] - [[r]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to double the first term's exponent before combining.
This adds every term instead of subtracting the last one.
This factors out the 2 across all three terms instead of only the first.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

solution: The natural logarithm and \( e^{x} \) are inverse functions, so \( \ln(e^{[[k]]}) = [[k]] = [[answer]] \) directly.
distractor_rationales: This is off by one from the correct value.
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This leaves the expression unevaluated instead of applying the inverse property.
This is off by two from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.
This is off by three from the correct value.

solution: Switching to a reciprocal base flips the sign of the logarithm: \( \log_{1/b}(M) = -\log_b(M) \). Since \( \log_{[[b]]}([[M]]) = [[p]] \), the answer is \( -[[p]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets that a reciprocal base flips the sign of the logarithm.
This is off by one in addition to missing the sign flip.
This inverts the exponent instead of negating it.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.
